login.subtitle: Your portal to understanding global electoral systems.

hub.title: Global Electoral Hub
hub.subtitle: Choose a system to explore its mechanics

systems.fptp_subhead: The simplest rule: most votes wins. Even if that's 30%.
systems.fptp_worksDesc: One vote per person. Candidate with most votes wins. No minimum threshold needed. Constituency-based (geographic). Winner-takes-all.
systems.fptp_shock: 🤯 In the 2005 UK election, Labour won a 66-seat majority with just 35.2% of the vote.
fptp_cons: Wasted votes — losing votes count for nothing
Can produce majority governments from minority votes

fptp_examples.desc: Britain invented FPTP. 650 constituencies. A party can win a majority of seats with just 35–40% of votes.
World's Largest. India uses FPTP for all 543 Lok Sabha seats. In 2019, BJP won 303 seats with 37.4% of the vote.
US House elections use FPTP. 435 districts. Gerrymandering is a major criticism.

systems.pr_subhead: Every vote counts. Your 15% becomes 15% of parliament.
systems.pr_worksDesc: Parties submit ranked lists of candidates. Voters vote for party, not person. Seats allocated proportionally. Used by Netherlands, Sweden, South Africa.
systems.pr_shock: 🤯 Israel has had 5 elections in 4 years due to constant coalition collapses under extreme PR.
pr_pros: More women and minority MPs elected
pr_cons: Often leads to coalition governments (slower, compromise-heavy)
Small extremist parties can gain seats
Voter influence over who gets elected is indirect

pr_examples.desc: The entire country is ONE constituency. Any party with 0.67% of votes gets a seat.
Uses PR with a 4% threshold to keep out fringe parties. Strong welfare state often linked to PR.
Extreme Case. PR is so proportional that small religious and nationalist parties hold enormous coalition leverage.

systems.two_subhead: Round 1: Everyone competes. Round 2: The final duel.
systems.two_worksDesc: Multiple candidates shown. If anyone gets >50%, they win. Usually top 2 advance to Round 2. One MUST get majority (50%+1).
systems.two_shock: 🤯 In 2002, France saw far-right Le Pen shock the nation by reaching Round 2, prompting massive tactical voting to defeat him 82% to 18%.
two_pros: Allows voters to express first choice without 'wasting' vote
Extremists rarely win (majority rejects them in Round 2)
two_cons: Voter fatigue and lower Round 2 turnout
Strategic voting still occurs in Round 1

two_examples.desc: Uses two rounds for both Presidential and Legislative elections.
Regularly goes to Round 2. In 2022, Lula vs Bolsonaro was won by just 1.8%.
Controversial Case. Uses a two-round system on paper but opposition faces restrictions.

systems.rcv_subhead: Don't just pick one. Rank them all. 1, 2, 3...
systems.rcv_worksDesc: Count all first-choice votes. If someone has >50%, they win. If not, eliminate last-place candidate and redistribute their votes. Repeat until one crosses 50%.
systems.rcv_shock: 🤯 Australians call it 'preferential voting'. Compulsory voting means 90%+ turnout.

rcv_examples.desc: Used preferential voting for House of Representatives since 1918.
Uses the Single Transferable Vote — a proportional version of RCV.
Adopted RCV in 2020. Breaks the two-party stranglehold.

systems.mixed_subhead: The best of both worlds? Two votes. Two methods. One parliament.
systems.mixed_worksDesc: Vote 1: Individual candidate (FPTP). Vote 2: Party vote (PR) to top up seats to match national vote share.
systems.mixed_shock: 🤯 Overhang seats caused the German Bundestag to grow to 736 seats in 2021.
mixed_pros: Small parties can enter via list vote
mixed_cons: Two classes of MPs (constituency vs list)
Complex — many voters don't understand both votes
Parties control list — removes some voter power

mixed_examples.desc: Created Mixed Member Proportional after WWII to prevent extremist majorities.
Switched from FPTP to MMP in 1996. Result: more diverse parliament.

systems.college_subhead: Sometimes the people vote for people who vote for the president.
systems.college_worksDesc: Citizens vote for Electors, not the President directly. Candidate needs majority of Electoral votes to win.
systems.college_shock: 🤯 In the 2000 and 2016 US Elections, the winner of the popular vote lost the presidency.
college_pros: Clear winner — avoids nationwide recount chaos
college_cons: Safe regions are essentially ignored in campaigns
Electors can theoretically vote against their state

college_examples.desc: Created in 1787. 538 electors. 48 states are winner-takes-all.
President is elected by an electoral college of all MPs and MLAs.
120 Cardinals elect the Pope. No campaigns allowed.

systems.direct_subhead: No parliament. No representatives. The people vote on the law itself.
systems.direct_worksDesc: Referendum: Govt asks citizens to vote Yes/No. Initiative: Citizens gather signatures to force a public vote.
systems.direct_shock: 🤯 Swiss citizens receive a massive booklet before every vote explaining all sides.

direct_examples.desc: The Global Leader. Holds 4 national referendums per year.
Allows citizens to place laws directly on the ballot.

systems.appointed_subhead: When There Is No Election. Power flows from institution.
systems.appointed_worksDesc: Absolute Monarchy, One-Party State, Military Junta, or Theocracy. Power is inherited, seized, or held by a single entity.
systems.appointed_shock: 🤯 In Myanmar (2021), the military rejected the election results and seized power.
appointed_cons: Human rights abuses with no consequence

appointed_examples.desc: One Party Elections. Only Communist Party candidates can run.
Absolute monarchy — the King rules by Islamic law.
Hybrid Theocracy. Unelected Supreme Leader has ultimate authority.

comparison.title: Global Comparison
comparison.subtitle: No perfect system exists. Every democracy is a compromise.

questions.q1: How important is it that every vote counts equally?
questions.q2: Should elections always produce stable single-party governments?
questions.q3: Should local areas have a dedicated representative in parliament?
questions.q4: Is it okay if a party wins power with less than 40% of votes?
questions.q5: Should citizens be able to directly vote on laws and referendums?
questions.q6: Is it important that small parties can enter parliament?
questions.q7: Should the winner always have 50%+ support to govern?
questions.q8: Is simplicity of counting more important than mathematical fairness?
